title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: malaria endemic areas, asymptomatic carriage malaria parasites occurs frequently detection malaria parasites blood films febrile individual necessarily indicate clinical malaria. case definition symptomatic malaria used widely endemic areas requires presence fever history fever together parasite density specific cutoff. parasite density equal higher cutoff point, fever considered due malaria. estimate sensitivity specificity cutoff point classification fever, without knowing true value fevers due malaria? Using attributable fraction, one can estimate expected number true cases due malaria positive predictive value associated given cutoff point, can estimate expected number true cases among fever cases parasite density higher equal selected cutoff point. Based values, rest 2x2 table can completed sensitivity, specificity negative predictive value. order estimate attributable fraction positive predictive value, follow method proposed Smith et al. (1) fitting logistic exponential model. presented afdx package R-software.

title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: data used example (malaria_df1) simulated data set seen frequently malaria cross-sectionals two main outcomes measured, presence fever history fever (fever column) measured parasite density parasites per μl\mu l (density column). head(malaria_df1) first 6 observations simulation, 2000 observations, 785 fever history fever 744 density malaria greater 0. total 437 fever malaria density higher 0. Distribution fevers density categories
code: library(afdx) #>  #> Attaching package: 'dplyr' #> The following objects are masked from 'package:stats': #>  #>     filter, lag #> The following objects are masked from 'package:base': #>  #>     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union #>  #> Attaching package: 'magrittr' #> The following object is masked from 'package:tidyr': #>  #>     extract #>  #> Attaching package: 'kableExtra' #> The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr': #>  #>     group_rows

title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: Smith et al (1) investigate different models describe risk fever due malaria. Given association linear, best model found logit exponential model: logit(πi)=β(xi)τlogit(\pi_i) = \beta(x_i)^\tau πi\pi_i probability fever observation ii xix_i parasite density observation ii. attributable fraction λ\lambda estimated : λ=(1/N)∑(Ri−1)/Ri\lambda = (1/N)\sum{_i}{(R_i - 1)/R_i} NN number fever cases, Ri=exp[β(xi)τ]R_i=exp[\beta(x_i)^\tau] case malaria defined case fever malaria density equal greater cutoff cc, ncn_c number fever cases accomplish definition proportion diagnosis cases attributable malaria λc\lambda_c estimated λc=(1/nc)∑idc,(Ri−1)/Ri\lambda_c = (1/n_c)\sum{_i}d_{c,}(R_i-1)/R_i dc,id_{c,} indicator value 1 fever case satisfies malaria case definition 0 otherwise Sensitivity cuttof cc defined : ncλc/Nλn_c\lambda_c / N\lambda, Specificity defined : 1−nc(1−λc)/N(1−λ)1-n_c(1-\lambda_c)/N(1-\lambda) Predictive positive value : ncλc/nc=λcn_c\lambda_c/n_c = \lambda_c Negative predicted value : (N(1−λ)−nc(1−λc))/(N−nc)(N(1-\lambda) - n_c(1-\lambda_c))/(N-n_c) example, 500 selected cutoff point, 420 cases fever density greater equal 500 total 785 fevers. model, estimated attributable fraction 42.63% proportion attributed malaria fevers equal greater 500 79.19% total number true malaria cases estimated Nλ=N\lambda= 334.7 number true malaria cases accomplish definition ncλcn_c\lambda_c = 332.6. full 2x2 table can filled corresponding sensitivity, specificity predictive values calculated. Case definition 500 cutoff

title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: adfx provide functions facilitate fitting logit exponential model estimate sensitivity, specificity, positive negative predictive values different cutoff points.

what: Graph of diagnostic characteristics for all cutoff points
title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: function make_cutoffs() find densities change number positives can used estimate characteristics cutoff points
code: cutoffs <-  make_cutoffs(malaria_df1$fever, malaria_df1$density) dxp <- senspec(fit, cutoffs[-1]) dxp %>%   data.frame(.) %>%   pivot_longer(-cutoff, names_to = "Indicator",values_to = "Value") %>%   ggplot(aes(x = cutoff, y = Value, color = Indicator, linetype = Indicator)) +   geom_line() +   scale_x_log10("Cutoff")

title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: receiver operative curve can estimated sensitivity specificity values
code: rocdf <-dxp %>%   data.frame(.) %>%   ## add the corners   bind_rows(     data.frame(       sensitivity= c(1,0),       specificity= c(0,1)     )   ) %>%   # generate the 1-specificity   mutate(`1-specificity` = 1 - specificity)     # make the graph   ggplot(rocdf, aes(x = `1-specificity`, y = sensitivity)) +   geom_line()+   ggtitle("ROC curve") # Estimate the area under the curve   library(DescTools)   AUC(rocdf$`1-specificity`, rocdf$sensitivity) #> Warning in regularize.values(x, y, ties, missing(ties), na.rm = na.rm): #> collapsing to unique 'x' values #> [1] 0.9695134

title: Attributable fraction using a logitexponetial model
text: Smith T, Schellenberg JA, Hayes R. Attributable fraction estimates case definitions malaria endemic areas. Stat Med. 1994 Nov 30;13(22):2345–58. DOI: 10.1002/sim.4780132206

title: Attributable fraction using a latent class model
text: malaria endemic areas, asymptomatic carriage malaria parasites occurs frequently detection malaria parasites blood films febrile individual necessarily indicate clinical malaria. case definition symptomatic malaria used widely endemic areas requires presence fever history fever together parasite density specific cutoff. parasite density equal higher cutoff point, fever considered due malaria. estimate sensitivity specificity cutoff point classification fever, without knowing true value fevers due malaria? Using attributable fraction, one can estimate expected number true cases due malaria positive predictive value associated given cutoff point, can estimate expected number true cases among fever cases parasite density higher equal selected cutoff point. order estimate attributable fraction positive predictive values, follow method proposed Vounatsou et al (1) fitting bayesian latent class model. latent class model several advantages logistic exponential model: make assumption shape risk fever, higher categories, higher risk fever little risk bias, provide direct estimation attributable fraction quantities like specificity sensitivity, allowing estimate directly confidence intervals, however attributable fraction can underestimated number categories high. presented afdx package R-software.

title: Attributable fraction using a latent class model
text: data used example (malaria_df1) simulated data set seen frequently malaria cross-sectionals two main outcomes measured, presence fever history fever (fever column) measured parasite density parasites per μl\mu l (density column). head(malaria_df1) first 6 observations simulation, 2000 observations, 785 fever history fever 744 density malaria greater 0. total 437 fever malaria density higher 0. Distribution fevers density categories

title: Attributable fraction using a latent class model
code: * TODO: Include more Details on the model  * TODO: Include Detail on the calculation of sensitivity, specificity and predictive values

what: Estimating the bayesian latent class model
title: Attributable fraction using a latent class model
text: adfx provide functions facilitate fitting bayesian latent class model using rjags package, user responsible setup appropriate bayesian workflow confirm convergence model. present one way many possibilities. use burn-1000 iterations monitor samples 4 chains 10000 iterations. function get_latent_model() provides string model can use rjags, Data model must provided list two vectors: n number subjects symptoms category m number subjects without symptoms model calculate data number categories (K) total number subjects group (Sn, Sm) Summary diagnostic characteristics selected cutoff points Attributable fraction: 0.419 95%CI(0.373, 0.463 )
code: model <- get_latent_model() cat(model) #>  #> data { #>   # Number of categories #>   K = length(n) #>   # Total events by group #>   Sn <- sum(n[]) #>   Sm <- sum(m[])  #> } #>  #> model { #>   for (i in 1:2){    #>     z0[i] <- (i-1)*0.0001 #>     phi0[i] <- theta[i]*z0[i]  #>   } #>   theta[1] <- 1-St #>   eltheta[1] ~ dgamma(1.0,1.0)  #>   theta[2] <- eltheta[2]/(1+Sr)  #>   eltheta[2] ~ dgamma(1.0E-3,1.0E-3)  #>   for (i in 3:K){  #>     phi0[i] <- theta[i]*z0[i]  #>    eltheta[i] ~ dgamma(1.0E-3,1.0E-3)  #>     theta[i] <- eltheta[i]/(1+Sr)  #>     z0[i] <- z0[i-1]/q[i] #>   } #>    #>   Sr <- sum(eltheta[2:K])  #>   St <- sum(theta[2:K])  #>   Sp <-sum(p0[])  #>   Sphi0 <- sum(phi0[])  #>   for (i in 1:K){ #>     phi[i] <- phi0[i]/Sphi0  #>     z[i] <- z0[i]/Sphi0  #>     q[i] ~ dunif(0.001,0.999) #>     p0[i] <- theta[i]*(1-lambda)+lambda*phi[i]  #>     p[i] <- p0[i]/Sp  #>     lami[i] <- lambda*phi[i]/p0[i] #>      #>     # Sens and specificity #>     sens[i] <- sum(phi[i:K]) #>     P[i] <- (1-lami[i])*p0[i] #>     spec[i] <- sum(P[1:i])/sum(P) #>     Q[i] <- lami[i]*p0[i] #>     ppv[i] <- sum(Q[i:K])/sum(p0[i:K]) #>     npv[i] = spec[i] * (1 - lambda) /(spec[i] * (1 - lambda) + ((1 - sens[i]) * lambda)) #>   } #>  #>   # m is afebrile (training sample) #>   m[1:K] ~ dmulti(theta[1:K], Sm)  #>    #>   # n is febrile (mixtured sample) #>   n[1:K] ~ dmulti(p[1:K], Sn)  #>    #>   # lambda is the fraction from the mixture that belong to the g2 #>   lambda~ dunif(0.00001,0.99999) #> } library(rjags) library(coda)  # compile the model af_latent <-   jags.model(     textConnection(get_latent_model()),     data = list(n = data$`n (fever)`,                 m = data$`m (no fever)`),     n.chains = 4,     n.adapt = 1000,     inits = list(       list(.RNG.name = "base::Wichmann-Hill", .RNG.seed = 1111),       list(.RNG.name = "base::Wichmann-Hill", .RNG.seed = 2222),       list(.RNG.name = "base::Wichmann-Hill", .RNG.seed = 3333),       list(.RNG.name = "base::Wichmann-Hill", .RNG.seed = 4444)     )   )  # Simulate the posterior latent_sim <-   coda.samples(     model = af_latent,     variable.names = c('lambda','sens','spec','ppv','npv'),     n.thinning = 5,     n.iter =  10000 )  # Extract and Analyze the posterior latent_sum <-  summary(latent_sim) latent_eff <-  effectiveSize(latent_sim) # reformat to present the results summary_table <-   data.frame(latent_sum[[1]]) %>%   bind_cols(data.frame(latent_sum[[2]])) %>%   mutate(varname = row.names(latent_sum[[1]])) %>%   mutate(cutoff  = c(NA, rep(cutoffs,4))) %>%   select(varname, cutoff,Mean, X2.5., X50., X97.5.,Naive.SE ) %>%   mutate(eff_size = floor(latent_eff)) %>%   filter(is.na(cutoff) | cutoff != 0)    mean_table <- summary_table %>%   rename(point = Mean) %>%   rename(lci = `X2.5.`) %>%   rename(uci = `X97.5.`) %>%   mutate(varname = gsub("\\[.*\\]","",varname)) %>%   filter(varname != "lambda") %>%   select(cutoff,varname, lci, uci,point) %>%   pivot_longer(-c("cutoff","varname"),names_to = "xxv", values_to = "value") %>%   unite("varx",varname,xxv ) %>%   pivot_wider(names_from = "varx", values_from = "value") %>%   select(cutoff,          sens_point,           sens_lci,           sens_uci,           spec_point,          spec_lci,           spec_uci,          ppv_point,           ppv_lci,           ppv_uci,          npv_point,          npv_lci,          npv_uci) %>%   rename(sensitivity = sens_point) %>%   rename(specificity = spec_point) %>%   rename(ppv = ppv_point) %>%   rename(npv = npv_point) %>%   mutate_if(is.numeric, round,3)  # Lambda corresponds to the attributable fraction afrow <-    summary_table %>%   filter(varname == "lambda") %>%    mutate_if(is.numeric, round,3)

title: Attributable fraction using a latent class model
text: Vounatsou P, Smith T, Smith AFM. Bayesian analysis two-component mixture distributions applied estimating malaria attributable fractions. title: Diagnosis Performance Using Attributable Fraction
text: R-package help estimation diagnosis performance (Sensitivity, Specificity, Positive predictive value, Negative predicted value) diagnostic test golden standard can’t measured can estimated using attributable fraction Two methods presented examples Malaria diagnosis, using maximum likelihood estimated logistic exponential model using bayesian latent class model. install package github use: devtools::install_github("johnaponte/afdx", build_manual = T, build_vignettes = T)

title: Template for the bayesian latent class model — get_latent_model
text: See: Smith T, Vounatsou P. Logistic regression latent class models estimating positivities diagnostic assays poor resolution. Communications Statistics - Theory Methods. 1997 Jan;26(7):1677–700. Vounatsou P, Smith T, Smith AFM. Bayesian analysis two-component mixture distributions applied estimating malaria attributable fractions. Journal Royal Statistical Society: Series C (Applied Statistics). 1998;47(4):575–87. Müller , Genton B, Rare L, Kiniboro B, Kastens W, Zimmerman P, et al. Three different Plasmodium species show similar patterns clinical tolerance malaria infection. Malar J. 2009;8(1):158. title: Template for the bayesian latent class model — get_latent_model
code:
{  get_latent_model() } #> [1] "
data {
  # Number of categories
  K = length(n)
  # Total events by group
  Sn <- sum(n[])
  Sm <- sum(m[]) 
}

model {
  for (i in 1:2){   
    z0[i] <- (i-1)*0.0001
    phi0[i] <- theta[i]*z0[i] 
  }
  theta[1] <- 1-St
  eltheta[1] ~ dgamma(1.0,1.0) 
  theta[2] <- eltheta[2]/(1+Sr) 
  eltheta[2] ~ dgamma(1.0E-3,1.0E-3) 
  for (i in 3:K){ 
    phi0[i] <- theta[i]*z0[i] 
   eltheta[i] ~ dgamma(1.0E-3,1.0E-3) 
    theta[i] <- eltheta[i]/(1+Sr) 
    z0[i] <- z0[i-1]/q[i]
  }
  
  Sr <- sum(eltheta[2:K]) 
  St <- sum(theta[2:K]) 
  Sp <-sum(p0[]) 
  Sphi0 <- sum(phi0[]) 
  for (i in 1:K){
    phi[i] <- phi0[i]/Sphi0 
    z[i] <- z0[i]/Sphi0 
    q[i] ~ dunif(0.001,0.999)
    p0[i] <- theta[i]*(1-lambda)+lambda*phi[i] 
    p[i] <- p0[i]/Sp 
    lami[i] <- lambda*phi[i]/p0[i]
    
    # Sens and specificity
    sens[i] <- sum(phi[i:K])
    P[i] <- (1-lami[i])*p0[i]
    spec[i] <- sum(P[1:i])/sum(P)
    Q[i] <- lami[i]*p0[i]
    ppv[i] <- sum(Q[i:K])/sum(p0[i:K])
    npv[i] = spec[i] * (1 - lambda) /(spec[i] * (1 - lambda) + ((1 - sens[i]) * lambda))
  }

  # m is afebrile (training sample)
  m[1:K] ~ dmulti(theta[1:K], Sm) 
  
  # n is febrile (mixtured sample)
  n[1:K] ~ dmulti(p[1:K], Sn) 
  
  # lambda is the fraction from the mixture that belong to the g2
  lambda~ dunif(0.00001,0.99999)
}
"
